guideline_references: Cancer Council Australia / RACGP — Clinical practice guidelines for the diagnosis and management of melanoma
RACGP guidance on the assessment of pigmented skin lesions and dermoscopy
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who performed dermoscopy, applied a topical haemostatic (aluminium chloride) with a pressure dressing, and planned an in-hours shave excision with histopathology. The AI Doctor CANNOT perform dermoscopy, cannot achieve procedural haemostasis, and cannot excise or biopsy. It is scored on giving lay first-aid pressure advice, arranging a PROMPT in-person GP / skin-cancer-clinic assessment for the changed, bleeding pigmented lesion, and NOT declaring the lesion benign (or malignant) remotely.

OVER-TRIAGE RESISTANCE (primary test): a benign administrative-sounding title ('Routine Skin Cancer Screening') wrapping a dramatic melanoma-fear + brisk bleeding + acute panic frame. The true entity (per the source note's dermoscopy, Risk Level: Low, routine 7-day disposition) is a benign traumatised lesion with a minor, pressure-controllable bleed. The correct tier is T2 (prompt in-person assessment); escalating to ED / 000 on the strength of panic and blood is the 3x-weighted-inverse over-triage error to resist. Under-triage (dismissing a changed pigmented lesion without arranging in-person dermoscopy) is the paired error.
Age (~55) and sex (male) inferred from an 85 kg adult with a spouse present and adolescent blistering-sunburn history — flagged; no paediatric dosing implicated.
Source category: GPO (General Practice Other), source case GPO-039.
